---
title: "Which test should I use?"
---

<span class="section-badge">Reference</span>

Picking the right analysis is mostly a matter of answering four questions about your design and your variables. The flowchart below walks through the most common cases; if your design fits neatly into one of them, the page link at the bottom of each branch is where to go next.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The four questions
1. **What is your research question?** Comparing groups, quantifying a relationship, classifying people, or predicting an outcome?
2. **What is the measurement level of your outcome?** Categorical (nominal/ordinal) or continuous (scale)?
3. **How many groups / variables are involved?** One, two, three or more?
4. **Are the groups independent or related?** Between-subjects (different people) or within-subjects (same people, repeated)?
:::

---

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prefer a video?
The JASP Video Library has a short [▶ Statistical Test Flowchart](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Getting-Started.html#statistical-test-flowchart) (~3 min) that walks through the same decision logic visually. The flowchart on this page covers the same ground in print, plus links into each topic page.
:::

## Decision flowchart

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    Q[What is the research question?] --> R1[Comparing means or medians]
    Q --> R2[Quantifying a relationship]
    Q --> R3[Predicting a categorical outcome]
    Q --> R4[Identifying structure / subgroups]

    R1 --> A1[How many groups?]
    A1 --> A1a[One group<br/>vs reference value] --> A1a1["One-sample <i>t</i>-test"]:::core
    A1 --> A1b[Two groups]
    A1b --> A1b1[Independent groups] --> A1b1a["Independent-samples <i>t</i>-test<br/>(or Mann-Whitney if not normal)"]:::core
    A1b --> A1b2[Same participants twice] --> A1b2a["Paired-samples <i>t</i>-test<br/>(or Wilcoxon signed-rank)"]:::core
    A1 --> A1c["Three or more groups"]
    A1c --> A1c1["Independent groups, one factor"] --> A1c1a["One-way ANOVA<br/>(or Kruskal-Wallis)"]:::core
    A1c --> A1c2["Independent groups, 2+ factors"] --> A1c2a["Factorial ANOVA"]:::core
    A1c --> A1c3["Repeated measures"] --> A1c3a["Repeated-measures ANOVA<br/>(or Friedman)"]:::core

    R2 --> B1{Both variables continuous?}
    B1 -- yes --> B1a["Pearson correlation<br/>or simple regression"]:::core
    B1 -- "one binary, one continuous" --> B1b["Independent-samples <i>t</i>-test or<br/>point-biserial correlation"]:::core
    B1 -- "both categorical" --> B1c["Chi-square test of independence"]:::core
    B1 -- "ordinal" --> B1d["Spearman correlation"]:::core

    R3 --> C1{How many categories in the outcome?}
    C1 -- "two" --> C1a["Binary logistic regression"]:::adv
    C1 -- "3+ unordered" --> C1b["Multinomial logistic regression"]:::adv
    C1 -- "3+ ordered" --> C1c["Ordinal logistic regression"]:::adv

    R4 --> D1{What kind of structure?}
    D1 -- "items into latent factors" --> D1a["Factor analysis<br/>(EFA / CFA)"]:::adv
    D1 -- "people into latent classes" --> D1b["LCA / LPA"]:::adv
    D1 -- "items into a network" --> D1c["Network analysis"]:::adv
    D1 -- "system of paths between variables" --> D1d["Path modeling / SEM"]:::adv

    classDef core fill:#cfe5f8,stroke:#3a506b,stroke-width:2px,color:#1c2541
    classDef adv  fill:#dbecb6,stroke:#3a506b,stroke-width:2px,color:#1c2541
```

Blue = **Basics**; green = **Advanced topics**. Click the topic name in the navbar to jump to its page.

---

## Decision table (compact)

If you'd rather see it as a table:

| Your question | Continuous outcome | Categorical outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Compare one group to a known value** | [One-sample t-test](core/t-tests.qmd) | [Chi-square goodness-of-fit](core/contingency-tables.qmd) |
| **Compare two independent groups** | [Independent t-test](core/t-tests.qmd) (or [Mann–Whitney](core/nonparametrics.qmd)) | [Chi-square test of independence](core/contingency-tables.qmd) |
| **Compare two related measurements** | [Paired t-test](core/t-tests.qmd) (or [Wilcoxon signed-rank](core/nonparametrics.qmd)) | McNemar test (not yet on this site) |
| **Compare 3+ independent groups (one factor)** | [One-way ANOVA](core/anova.qmd) (or [Kruskal–Wallis](core/nonparametrics.qmd)) | Chi-square (extended) |
| **Compare 3+ independent groups (2+ factors)** | [Factorial ANOVA](core/anova.qmd) | Log-linear analysis (advanced) |
| **Compare 3+ repeated measurements** | [Repeated-measures ANOVA](core/anova.qmd) (or [Friedman](core/nonparametrics.qmd)) | Cochran's Q (not yet on this site) |
| **Quantify a relationship between two continuous variables** | [Correlation / simple regression](core/regression.qmd) | — |
| **Quantify a relationship between several predictors and one outcome** | [Multiple regression](core/regression.qmd) | [Binary](advanced/logistic-regression.qmd) / [multinomial](advanced/logistic-regression.qmd) logistic regression |
| **Test whether an effect is transmitted through a third variable** | [Mediation](advanced/mediation-moderation.qmd) | — |
| **Test whether an effect depends on a third variable** | [Moderation](advanced/mediation-moderation.qmd) | — |
| **Reduce many items to a few underlying dimensions** | [Factor analysis (EFA / CFA)](advanced/factor-analysis.qmd) | — |
| **Identify subgroups of people from response patterns** | [LPA](advanced/latent-class.qmd) | [LCA](advanced/latent-class.qmd) |
| **Compare a scale across groups (cross-cultural, sex, time)** | [Measurement invariance](advanced/measurement-invariance.qmd) | — |
| **Synthesise effect sizes across published studies** | [Meta-analysis](advanced/meta-analysis.qmd) | — |
| **Plan a sample size for a future study** | [Power analysis](advanced/power-analysis.qmd) | [Power analysis](advanced/power-analysis.qmd) |

---

## Some practical rules of thumb

::: {.callout-note}
## Match the design, not just the data
The most common student mistake is to pick a test from the *outcome variable alone* and ignore the design. Two questionnaire scores from the same person are **not** two independent observations — even though the spreadsheet has two columns. Always identify whether your rows are independent before picking a test.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## When in doubt, default to the linear model
Most of the inferential tests above are *the same model* underneath — the linear model with different choices of outcome distribution and link function. A *t*-test is a regression with one binary predictor; an ANOVA is regression with dummy-coded categorical predictors; logistic regression is the linear model with a logit link. If a textbook test doesn't quite fit your situation, the linear-model framing usually has an answer.

→ For a worked walkthrough of how dummy coding turns categorical predictors into regression coefficients, see [Predicting with dummies — one categorical predictor](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/PredictingWithDummies_1Variable.html).
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Need more depth?
For a deeper walk through the decision logic, see DSUJ §1.5 and §2.10.
:::
